Her defense of the administration’s actions during this time led to speculation that she was being considered for a larger role, with *The Washington Post* reporting on May 26 that she was a possible replacement for Spicer. This speculation grew as Sanders became the face of the administration’s response to the Comey firing, showcasing her ability to handle high-pressure situations and fueling rumors of a senior staff shake-up within the White House communications team.

As of the census of 2010, there were 6,069 people, 2,702 households, and 1,356 families residing in the city. The population density was 776.1 inhabitants per square mile (299.7/km2). There were 2,996 housing units at an average density of 383.1 per square mile (147.9/km2). The racial makeup of the city was 96.6% White, 0.9% African American, 0.7% Native American, 0.3% Asian, 0.5% from other races, and 1.0% from two or more races. Hispanic or Latino of any race were 2.3% of the population.

As a constituent republic of Yugoslavia, Bosnia and Herzegovina was among its poorer regions, with a centrally planned economy and inefficient agricultural practices. The Bosnian War (1992–1995) devastated the country, resulting in the deaths of approximately 100,000 people and the displacement of over two million. The war caused material damage estimated at $200 billion, with GDP plummeting by 80%. Post-war, Bosnia faced immense challenges, including rebuilding its economy, transitioning from communism to a free market, and addressing high unemployment, which remained around 40% officially, though unofficial estimates were lower. Despite over $5 billion in international aid, Bosnia’s GDP remained less than half its pre-war size, and over 60% of the population lived in poverty. By 2017, Bosnia’s debt stood at $12 billion, reflecting ongoing struggles with economic revitalization and structural reforms.

Akeytsu is developed by the company Nukeygara, which was co-founded in 2014 in Lyon, France, by Aurélien Charrier. He is an animator with 12 years experience in the video game industry. His aim with Akeytsu was to build a feature set that was intuitive and easy to use. The beta version was found to be a simple focused tool concentrated on animation. Akeytsu was launched on Steam engine in 2016.

The Williams Rugby Football Club (WRFC) was founded in 1959 by David Coughlin after studying abroad in England. Supported by its first coach, Dr. Bruce Williams, who learned rugby in South Africa, the club entered the Eastern Rugby Union (ERU) that fall. In 1960, the WRFC won the ERU Championship, sharing the Carling Cup with Dartmouth, finishing with a 5-0-1 record. Increased support and recognition from the College came in 1961, when the team gained access to the Aldrich-Dexter Athletic Complex and adopted authentic rugby kits, solidifying its presence on campus.

Aparados da Serra National Park was established in 1959 with an original area of 13,141 hectares, later reduced to 10,250 hectares in 1972 through Federal Decree No. 70,296. The adjacent Serra Geral National Park, created in 1992, spans 17,300 hectares, forming a contiguous conservation area. Despite these efforts, ParksWatch has highlighted that the park's current size remains insufficient for effective biodiversity protection. According to Duke University's Center for Tropical Conservation, the park aims to preserve its unique ecosystems for scientific, cultural, and recreational purposes.

The Cleveland Cavaliers began play as an NBA expansion team in the 1970 season. After six losing seasons, they won their first division title in 1976 under head coach Bill Fitch, who was named NBA Coach of the Year. Key players included Jim Chones and Campy Russell. That season is remembered for the "Miracle of Richfield," when the Cavaliers made their first playoff appearance and defeated the Washington Bullets in a dramatic seven-game Eastern Conference Semifinals, highlighted by three last-second game-winning shots. The decisive Game 7 ended with a thrilling victory for the Cavs, as they advanced to the Eastern Conference Finals. However, their playoff run concluded with a six-game series loss to the Boston Celtics. The Miracle of Richfield remains one of the most iconic moments in franchise history, celebrated for the team's resilience and the electrifying atmosphere at the Richfield Coliseum.

Despite the loss, Taylor rebounded by entering the first-ever Rey de Voladores tournament on April 22, 2007, defeating the likes of PAC, Ricochet, Retail Dragon en route to the finals of the tournament, where he defeated Lince Dorado. However, problems in the Kings of Wrestling arose as Chris Hero, the man he replaced at King of Trios, had split the Kings of Wrestling stable in two, with Hero, Sweeney, and Claudio Castagnoli comprising the Kings of Wrestling while Team F.I.S.T. (Taylor, Akuma, and Icarus) once again became a separate team. At the Aniversario! event in May, the Kings and F.I.S.T. met in a six-man tag team match, which F.I.S.T. won. Despite losing, the Kings reunited with F.I.S.T. and the Kings of Wrestling became the top stable in Chikara, later recruiting Mitch Ryder, Max Boyer and Shayne Hawke to their ranks. Using this momentum, Taylor went on to win the Young Lions Cup V, beating Cabana Man Dan, Lince Dorado, MosCOW the Communist Bovine, Player Uno, Ice Cream, Jr. and Amigo Suzuki on the first night and Arik Cannon in the semifinals on the second night, before beating longtime rival Ricochet in the finals.

Roger A. Jourdain was born in 1912 and received his education locally before dedicating his life to public service. As a prominent leader and advocate for American Indian sovereignty, he played a key role in reforming the Red Lake Nation's government. A leading member of the Young Men’s General Advisory Council, Jourdain supported a revised constitution that established an elected tribal council, fostering greater self-governance. In 1959, he was elected chair of the Red Lake Tribal Council, a position he held until 1990, overseeing significant community development projects.

The Australian leg of Slipknot's *All Hope Is Gone World Tour* marked the first time the band brought their full production, described by percussionist Shawn Crahan as "the real set, the real deal," to Australian audiences. Traveling with over 11 tons of equipment, the band showcased hydraulic drum risers, percussion rigs, pyrotechnics, and elaborate stage setups. Prior to the first show, lead vocalist Corey Taylor, known as Slipknot #9, sprained his thumb while skydiving. Despite the injury, Taylor performed without issue, delivering the high-energy performances Slipknot is renowned for.

Jourdain was born on July 27, 1912. He received an eighth-grade education and worked as a machine operator, participating in the construction of the Alaska Highway and the regional airport in Bemidji, Minnesota. Additionally, Jourdain was a leading member of the "Young Man's Council", a group of Red Lake citizens desirous of governmental reform. A revised constitution supported by the Young Man's Council resulted in the replacement of Red Lake's traditionally-selected governing body by an elected tribal council, of which Jourdain was elected chair in 1959.

Marcel Parent was elected to the National Assembly of Quebec in a 1984 by-election, representing the division of Sauvé. He won the seat previously held by the Parti Québécois, as a member of the Quebec Liberal Party. At the time, the Parti Québécois was in government. Parent was re-elected in the 1985 provincial election, in which the Liberals, led by Robert Bourassa, won a majority government. On February 27, 1986, Parent was appointed chair of the province's education committee, a position he held until August 8, 1989.

The Rawei family, known for their international tours, began their travels in Australia in 1895, presenting the show *Land of the Maori*. This performance featured Hine Taimoa alongside other family members, showcasing Maori culture. In 1898, an Australian reporter praised Hine Taimoa’s voice as “sweet and musical” and her speech as “eloquent and refined.” The family later toured England in 1897, continuing to captivate audiences with their cultural performances and storytelling, which highlighted their heritage and traditions.

A study done in 2007 found that infants born to Mexican-immigrant women in the United States had a 10% lower mortality rate than infants born to non-Hispanic women in the United States. This research further support for the Hispanic paradox. Although Palestine refugee communities face socioeconomic hardship and have high fertility rates, their infant and childhood mortality rates are among the lowest in the Arab world. The causes of neonatal mortality among Palestine refugees are proportionally similar to those found in the most developed regions of the world. Non-communicable diseases are the leading causes of infant, and particularly neonatal deaths, among Palestine refugees, as they are among industrialized countries in Europe and North America.

Some Catholic saints and theologians have had sometimes conflicting ideas about purgatory beyond those adopted by the Catholic Church, reflecting or contributing to the popular image, which includes the notions of purification by actual fire, in a determined place and for a precise length of time. Paul J. Griffiths notes: "Recent Catholic thought on purgatory typically preserves the essentials of the basic doctrine while also offering second-hand speculative interpretations of these elements". Thus Joseph Ratzinger wrote: "Purgatory is not, as Tertullian thought, some kind of supra-worldly concentration camp where man is forced to undergo punishment in a more or less arbitrary fashion. Rather it is the inwardly necessary process of transformation in which a person becomes capable of Christ, capable of God, and thus capable of unity with the whole communion of saints".

Eileen Kramer was born on 8 November 1914 in Mosman Bay, Sydney, and grew up with her brother. Her father, a car salesman, began showing signs of alcoholism when she was about 10. At 13, her mother relocated with the children to Coogee, where she worked as a store detective at Farmers, a department store on George Street.

Raptor is a family of rocket engines developed by SpaceX exclusively for use in Starship and Super Heavy. It is fueled by liquid oxygen and methane, burning them in a highly efficient full-flow staged combustion power cycle. It is the third engine that uses such a cycle and the first that powered a flight vehicle. The Raptor engine uses methane as the fuel of choice over other rocket propellants, for reasons including the facts that methane burns 'cleaner', produces less soot, and can be directly synthesized from carbon dioxide and water. These properties make the engine highly practical, efficient and long-lasting.

The SAMOS 3 launch was a complete disaster. SAMOS 3 lifted from PALC 1-1 on 9 September 1961 at 00:00:00 GMT, was a US Air Force first-generation photo surveillance satellite with 7 instruments on board  but the Atlas booster's engines shut down after the rocket had ascended about 29 cm and it fell back onto the pad in an enormous fireball. Postflight investigation found that an umbilical on PALC 1-1 had not detached on time, resulting in the booster being switched from internal to external power, but since the power umbilicals on the pad had already detached, the result was a complete loss of electrical power to all Atlas systems. The accident necessitated improved procedures to umbilical lanyard installation, and modifications to the launcher system to prevent the hold-down arms from releasing the launch vehicle until all umbilicals had detached.

The terrestrial biosphere encompasses all organic carbon in living and dead organisms, including plants, animals, and microbes, as well as carbon stored in soils. Approximately 2,000 gigatons of carbon (GtC) are stored in the terrestrial biosphere, with soils holding two to three times more carbon than the atmosphere and four times more than living plants. Organic carbon, primarily in the form of sugars, starches, and cellulose, dominates the biosphere, while a smaller fraction exists as inorganic carbon, such as calcium carbonate in soils. Organic carbon is essential for life, forming the structural and energy basis of organisms. Terrestrial autotrophs acquire carbon dioxide directly from the atmosphere for photosynthesis.

Oreal Perras debuted in 1963 as Red McNulty, an Irish rogue character billed from Dublin, Ireland, distinguished by an eyepatch. Wrestling primarily in the Toronto area, he left his regular job to pursue wrestling full-time, gaining experience in northwestern Canada. This eventually led to his first international wrestling trip to Japan.

Hibbertia bicarpellata is a small shrub typically growing to a height of up to 0.3 m. Its branches are wiry and covered with a combination of star-shaped hairs and longer, simple hairs. The leaves are linear to narrow elliptic, measuring 4–10 mm long and 0.5–1 mm wide, with a petiole about 0.5 mm long. The flowers are arranged singly on the ends of branches or in leaf axils, each on a peduncle 3–8 mm long. The bracts are linear to lance-shaped, 2–3 mm long, and hairy. The sepals are lance-shaped, with the outer lobes 3.5–4 mm long and the inner lobes slightly longer at 4–4.5 mm. The petals are yellow, egg-shaped with the narrower end towards the base, and 4–5 mm long. There are six to eight stamens arranged around the two carpels, each carpel containing two ovules. Flowering occurs from August to October.

Carol Moseley Braun began her political career in 1978 when she was elected to the Illinois House of Representatives, becoming the first African American woman to serve as assistant majority leader. Recognized as a champion for liberal social causes, she proposed a moratorium on the death penalty in 1984, highlighting its racial disparities. That same year, she successfully sued her own party and the state of Illinois in the landmark reapportionment case *Crosby v. State Board of Elections*, advocating for African American, Hispanic, and female citizens. During her decade in the Illinois House, she focused on civil rights, education reform, and anti-discrimination legislation. In 1988, she was elected Cook County Recorder of Deeds, becoming the first African American to hold an executive position in Cook County government.

"Flash," a single by Perfume, was sold digitally on platforms such as iTunes, recochoku, and A! MUSIC. The digital jacket was drawn by Yoshiyuki Sadamoto. It was included on their album *Cosmic Explorer*, where it was reworked into a single mix. The first limited edition of the album featured additional content, including a DVD with music videos and behind-the-scenes footage.

The Rawei family toured internationally, singing, telling stories, and presenting a magic lantern show of hand-coloured scenes from Māori life in New Zealand. They started their travels in Australia in 1893, with a show called "Land of the Maori" that included Hine's sister, Hari Taimoa. "Her voice is full and beautiful, and her speech the essence of refinement," noted an 1898 Australian reporter of Hine Taimoa. The also made appearances in England, in 1897.

Brian Reynolds made his first game sale with *Quest 1*, featured on the August 1981 cover of *SoftSide* magazine. As a high school student, he was a SysOp on Randolph School's PDP-11 mainframe computer. He graduated summa cum laude from the University of the South in 1990 and briefly pursued graduate studies in Philosophy at UC Berkeley.

The Arlington Boulevard Trail is a set of discontinuous trails and on-road bike routes, 11 miles in length, on both sides of Arlington Boulevard. The trail runs from N. Rhodes Street in Arlington County to Fairview Park Drive in Falls Church. It has been proposed that the trail be extended to Pickets Road in Fairfax Virginia.

The Security Branch was responsible for extrajudicial killings, especially of anti-apartheid activists (see below). The TRC concluded that its officers had carried out many political assassinations, "frequently with the authorisation or involvement of senior Security Branch personnel." The Branch carried out some very prominent assassinations, such as those of Griffiths Mxenge, Ruth First, and the Cradock Four. It used gunshots, various kinds of explosives, and, in at least one case – that of Black Consciousness activist Siphiwe Mthimkulu – thallium. In 1989, the Branch also carried out the attempted murder of Frank Chikane, at Vlok's instruction, by lacing his underwear with a nerve agent. Targets were sometimes killed in or abducted from neighbouring countries, especially Swaziland, and were often ambushed based on information obtained from the Branch's informants.

On 23 March 2019, the Islamic State lost its final significant territory in Syria during the Battle of Baghuz, marking the end of its self-declared "caliphate." The Kurdish-led Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF), backed by the United States, the United Kingdom, and France, declared victory, describing the area as an "apocalyptic wasteland" and announcing that IS had been "totally eliminated" in Syria. British Prime Minister Theresa May praised the "immense courage" of the UK Armed Forces and their allies, stating that the liberation of the last Daesh-held territory would not have been possible without their efforts. She reaffirmed the UK's commitment to protecting its people and allies from the ongoing threat posed by IS. Major General Ghika confirmed that Operation Shader would continue, emphasizing the need to support Iraqi security forces and counter IS, which had transitioned to a guerrilla insurgency despite its territorial defeat.

Multi-story buildings occupied by rental apartments, known as a rab' (plural ribā' or urbu), became common in the Mamluk period and continued to be a feature of the city's housing during the later Ottoman period. This type of housing was a relatively unique to Cairo at the time. These apartments were often laid out as multi-story duplexes or triplexes. They were sometimes attached to caravanserais (wikalas) where the two lower floors were for commercial and storage purposes and the upper floors above were rented out to tenants. Even in the case of these wikala-rab' combinations, the apartment complexes had their own street entrances separate from the commercial complex below. The oldest partially-preserved example of this type of structure is the Wikala of Amir Qawsun, which was built before 1341, but multiple later examples have survived from later centuries, either as part of caravanserais or as independent buildings. Residential buildings in Cairo were in turn organized into close-knit neighbourhoods called a harat, which in many cases had gates that could be closed off at night or during disturbances.

Capacitor discharge (CD) stud welding differs from drawn arc stud welding primarily in its suitability for thinner base materials and smaller-diameter studs, as well as its faster weld times, typically completed in 0.01 seconds. Unlike drawn arc welding, CD stud welding does not require flux or shielding gas, simplifying the process and reducing costs. The rapid discharge of electrical energy creates a precise arc, minimizing heat buildup and preventing warping, discoloration, or reverse side marking, making it ideal for thin metals as light as 0.020" (0.5mm). CD stud welding is commonly used in applications requiring clean, aesthetically pleasing welds, such as automotive, electronics, and lightweight manufacturing. In contrast, drawn arc stud welding is better suited for larger fasteners and thicker, rougher base materials in heavy-duty construction and fabrication.

Over the last two decades recruiting international athletes has become a growing trend among NCAA institutions. For example, most German athletes outside of Germany are based at US universities. For many European athletes, the American universities are the only option to pursue an academic and athletic career at the same time. Many of these students come to the US with high academic expectations and aspirations.

While SNR is commonly quoted for electrical signals, it can be applied to any form of signal, for example isotope levels in an ice core, biochemical signaling between cells, or financial trading signals. SNR is sometimes used metaphorically to refer to the ratio of useful information to false or irrelevant data in a conversation or exchange. For example, in online discussion forums and other online communities, off-topic posts and spam are regarded as noise that interferes with the signal of appropriate discussion.

Beginning in 1947 Bethel became the site of "pioneering work" in organizational development. Led by psychologist Kurt Lewin and, eventually, colleagues like Ken Benne and Ron Lippitt, research was conducted that helped lay the foundation for organizational development's theory and practice. T-groups and sensitivity training were refined to apply behavioral science to individual self-awareness and group functioning, in the service of organizational efficiency and productivity. The National Training Laboratories (NTL) was organized by Lewin to lead this work, with much or it conducted during summer seasons at Gould Academy. These seasonal research and training sessions were well attended, attracting participants from across the country and around the world. Increasingly, NTL became an important part of Bethel's economy, especially as the traditional wood product mill industries went into decline. Although NTL moved its headquarters to Alexandria, Virginia in 2008, it continues to maintain a small summer legacy presence in Bethel.

The first NC 67 was an original state highway, founded in 1921, that traversed from Conover to Sparta. Originally, the southern terminus of NC 67 was located at NC 75 in Taylorsville. NC 67 then travelled north along a topsoil, sand-clay, or gravel roadway to Moravian Falls where it intersected NC 18. From Moravian Falls, NC 67 travelled northeast along a topsoil, sand-clay, or gravel road, intersecting NC 60 in Wilkesboro. NC 67 continued north-northwest from Wilkesboro through Wilkes County. In the northern part of Wilkes County, the roadway became a graded road and then entered Alleghany County. As NC 67 approached Sparta from the southwest, the roadway became unimproved. The original northern terminus of NC 67 was located at NC 26 in Sparta.

The animation for *What If... the World Lost Its Mightiest Heroes?* was provided by Marvel Studios, with Ryan Meinerding, Marvel Studios' Head of Visual Development, collaborating with Bryan Andrews to develop the series' cel-shaded animation style. Meinerding's iconic vision shaped the episode's look, though animation quality varied across episodes. Concept art for the episode, included in *Marvel Studios: The Art of Ryan Meinerding*, was released online by Marvel alongside the episode's debut on Disney+.

The Kewanee A's played minor league home games at Northeast Park in 1948 and 1949. The park's capacity was 2,500 in 1948 and 4,000 in 1949. Located at 1200 N Main St., Kewanee, Illinois, Northeast Park now hosts various recreational activities.

In 2013, Zach Hill was reported to be working on an original feature film, with late actress Karen Black confirmed for a leading role. However, Black passed away in August 2013 before the project could be completed. A 15-minute excerpt of the footage, titled *Bottomless Pit*, was uploaded to the Death Grips YouTube channel in October 2015. It has been rumored that either Death Grips or Hill's side-project, the I.L.Y's, would contribute to the film's soundtrack.

The Herndon Home, located at 587 University Place NW atop Diamond Hill, one of Atlanta's highest points, is a National Historic Landmark adjacent to the Vine City neighborhood. Designed in the Classical Revival style with Beaux-Arts influences, its grand entrance portico is supported by paired Corinthian columns, and the intricate etched glass windows add to its elegance. The mansion is capped with a flat roof, which, as of 2000, was reported to be in good condition. Inside, the home features a blend of wood, stone, and plaster materials, with hand-painted murals in the main living rooms narrating the Herndon family history. These murals, along with custom-designed antique furniture, reflect the home’s rich artistic and cultural heritage.

As of the census of 2010, there were 260 people, 120 households, and 75 families living in the town. The population density was 722.2 inhabitants per square mile (278.8/km2). There were 149 housing units at an average density of 413.9 per square mile (159.8/km2). The racial makeup of the town was 98.5% White, 1.2% Native American, and 0.4% from two or more races.

On July 1, 2015, the Tampa Bay Lightning signed free agent forward Erik Condra to a three-year contract with an average annual value of $1.25 million. Condra, who had spent the previous five seasons with the Ottawa Senators, was originally drafted by the Senators in the seventh round of the 2006 NHL Draft. In his final season with Ottawa, Condra recorded nine goals and 14 assists for 23 points in 68 games. Known for his reliability as a penalty killer and his ability to contribute as a third- or fourth-line player, Condra was also familiar to Lightning head coach Jon Cooper, who had coached him during their time in junior hockey. Later that same day, the Lightning also signed defenseman Matt Taormina to a one-year, two-way contract worth $600,000 at the NHL level and $200,000 in the AHL. Taormina, who had played 76 games with the Worcester Sharks in the 2014–15 AHL season, scoring 11 goals and 38 points, had previously spent time in the Lightning organization during the 2013–14 season. Both signings bolstered Tampa Bay’s depth as the team prepared for the 2015–16 campaign.

After leaving RNK Split in November 2016, Elis Bakaj returned to Albania and began training with Tirana to maintain his form. In an interview, he expressed his desire to play abroad but stated that if he stayed in Albania, Tirana would be his only choice. He continued training with the club in January 2017 and reached an agreement on January 26 for a contract lasting until the end of the 2016–17 season. However, FIFA blocked the transfer due to Tirana's unpaid debts, which prohibited the club from signing players during the January transfer window, leaving Bakaj unable to join officially.

On 26 May 2012, following the Houla massacre, British Foreign Secretary William Hague called for an urgent session of the United Nations Security Council, urging the Assad regime to grant "full and immediate" access to Houla for UN monitors. Special Envoy Kofi Annan demanded the regime cease all military operations. Meanwhile, opposition groups expressed their loss of faith in the Security Council, citing Russia’s continued support for Damascus, including vetoing resolutions against Assad, despite growing criticism and Moscow’s increasingly critical stance toward the Syrian government.

George FitzRoy, Earl of Euston, married Lady Dorothy Boyle on 10 October 1741. She was the elder daughter of Richard Boyle, 4th Earl of Cork and 3rd Earl of Burlington, and Lady Dorothy Savile, Countess of Burlington and Countess of Cork. Lady Dorothy Boyle, described as "a girl of remarkable beauty and sweetness" in the History of Parliament, tragically died on 2 May 1742, just seven months after their marriage. Her mother lamented that her death was caused by the mistreatment she endured from her husband, who was notorious for his cruelty. George FitzRoy died childless in 1747.

Lev L. Spiro earned dual bachelor's degrees in Political Science and Communication Arts from the University of Wisconsin–Madison before obtaining a master's degree in Film Production from the University of Texas at Austin. He adapted James Lee Burke's short story *The Convict* into a film and directed a film adaptation of Robert Louis Stevenson's *The Suicide Club*, starring Jonathan Pryce and Paul Bettany.

Sergei Stepashin was born on March 2, 1952, in Lushunkou, China, now known as Lüshun, China. He graduated from the Higher Political School of the USSR Ministry of the Interior in 1973, the Lenin Military-Political Academy in 1981, and the Finance Academy in 2002. Stepashin holds a Doctor of Law degree, is a Professor, and has the military rank of Colonel-General.

Greeny is known for its unique tone, which has been described as "magic" by numerous publications and other musicians. The guitar is equipped with PAF pickups. Early in his career, Green attempted to emulate Eric Clapton's tone by removing the neck pickup. Eventually he put in back in, but accidentally backwards. This gave the guitar its "distinctly nasal" out-of-phase tone when the pickup selector switch is in the middle position. Kirk Hammett described it as sounding like "a Strat through a 100-watt Marshall stack". The neck pickup also features a flipped alnico magnet, thought to be a factory error or an aftermarket modification. The guitar's neck has also been broken twice, which Hammett feels has also affected its tone. Peter Green, however, felt that there was nothing particularly special about the guitar or his sound. He also stated that he didn't know what "out-of-phase" even meant.

As of 2001 India census, Jaitwara had a population of 8,903. Males constitute 51% of the population and females 49%. Jaitwara has an average literacy rate of 59%, lower than the national average of 59.5%: male literacy is 69%, and female literacy is 50%. In Jaitwara, 18% of the population is under 6 years of age. It is located near the border of Uttar Pradesh. The nearest city is Satna.

The cap is 5–12 cm (2–4+3⁄4 in) wide, pale to orange-yellow with grayish brownish or reddish tomentum, and viscid while fresh. The fibrillose-scaly surface of the cap helps distinguish it from other species in the genus. The tubes are yellow and become blue when bruised. The stipe is 4–11 cm (1+5⁄8–4+3⁄8 in) tall and 1–3 cm wide, grandular dotted and the color is similar to the cap. The cap is scaly and has fibrillose. The spores are brownish when they are young. The spore print is dark olive brown to brown. The species stains fingers blue. It has no veil.

Sam Donahue (1918–1974), a tenor saxophonist from Detroit, Michigan, formed his first band at the age of 15 in 1933. He played with notable bands, including those of Gene Krupa, Benny Goodman, and Harry James, before joining Artie Shaw's Navy Band during World War II, taking over leadership in 1944. After the war, Donahue led a civilian band that recorded for V-Discs and featured trumpeters like Doc Severinsen and vocalists Jo Stafford and Frances Wayne. The band dissolved in 1951 when Donahue re-enlisted in the Navy during the Korean War. Known for his swing-era contributions, Donahue's career spanned both military and civilian music leadership.

On 13 May 2011, Giles Coren sparked controversy by joking about a privacy injunction on Twitter, referring to a footballer at the center of the super-injunction as a "fucking shit midfielder" and "a very ugly married man" involved with a "gold-digging flopsie." The following day, on 14 May, Coren tweeted about Gareth Barry, implying insider knowledge by suggesting Barry might score in a match and placing a "tiny fiver" bet at 22-1 odds. He also commented on Barry being "remarkably relaxed" and later joked about him being "pulled off." This tweet was subsequently deleted but archived. Coren’s posts, which appeared to flout legal restrictions and mock the injunctions, drew criticism and raised questions about potential contempt of court.

"Little Istanbul," an area in South Paterson centered on Main Street, is the largest Turkish American enclave in the United States. This vibrant community, also referred to as "Little Ramallah," reflects the diverse cultural fabric of Paterson, New Jersey, which is home to the second-largest Arab-American community in the U.S. after Dearborn, Michigan. Alongside Turkish Americans, the area includes Arab Americans, particularly Palestinian Americans, with an estimated Arab American population of 15,000 in 2015. Signs in Arabic, halal businesses, and cultural programs, such as the Arabic language classes run by the Arab American Civic Association, highlight the neighborhood's Middle Eastern influence. Paterson is also part of Passaic County, one of the top Arab ethnic enclaves in the country.

For small or backyard growers, managing cherry leaf spot requires diligent sanitation practices, with the removal and destruction of all fallen leaf debris being essential. The fungus *Blumeriella jaapii* overwinters in infected leaves on the ground, and removing these leaves significantly reduces the primary inoculum available in spring, thereby lowering the apparent infection rate. Studies suggest that applying nitrogen to fallen leaves can enhance decomposition, further reducing spring infections. Adding straw mulch after leaf removal helps retain soil moisture and suppress weeds but does not directly impact the disease. While leaf litter removal is effective for small-scale growers, it is often impractical for large commercial operations due to the labor and costs involved. These measures are critical for breaking the disease cycle and protecting cherry trees in smaller orchards or backyard settings.

Randall first contested the since-abolished seat of Henley Beach as a Liberal at the 1977 election. Randall won the seat as a Liberal at the 1979 election and from the 1980 Norwood by-election Randall was the one-term Tonkin Liberal government's most marginal seat holder on a one percent margin before losing the seat at the 1982 election. He again contested the seat as a Liberal at the 1989 election. The seat of Colton was created at the 1991 redistribution to replace the abolished seat of Henley Beach. Randall once again contested Liberal preselection, this time for the 1993 election, but was defeated by preselection winner Steve Condous who went on to win the seat. Randall contested Colton as an independent.

The ideal candidate site of proposed wind power projects is located approximately 300 meter masl in the mining area near Carrascal. It is accessible from a highway under perpetual reconstruction and further made accessible by exploration roads constructed by mining companies. The terrain is mostly flat, with minimal vegetation, causing minimal turbulence to wind flow in the area. It overlooks the Philippine Sea from the southeast and the north-west directions. Some permanent deformations of small trees indicate that the wind typically comes from the north-east direction. Ground measurements yielded wind speeds of 8–11 m/s, which, by industry standards has excellent wind power potential. However, further validation studies of at least one year of wind data gathered from the site is nonexistent. Transmission lines of the local cooperative pass through this wind power candidate site, making it more attractive for wind and/ solar energy development to alleviate badly needed energy infrastructure to sustain large scale industrialization and its lofty bid to become the northernmost "city" of Surigao del Sur.

On review aggregator website Rotten Tomatoes, the film holds an approval rating of 89%, with an average rating of 7.2/10 based on 44 reviews. Its critical consensus reads, "It takes its time coming together, but the quietly effective Paddleton pulls off a tricky tonal balancing act, thanks largely to the strengths of its well-chosen leads." Metacritic, which uses a weighted average, assigned a score of 70 out of 100 based on 17 critics, indicating "generally favorable reviews."

Higher education in Venezuela operates under significant government oversight, with universities granted limited autonomy for self-governance. While they can supplement their budgets through private donations or royalties from intellectual property, the Ministry of Education retains ultimate control. The Minister oversees key aspects, including budgets, curriculum planning, infrastructure, research, and policy implementation. Autonomous universities, traditionally independent, face challenges to their institutional freedom, with funding often restricted if they resist government control. The Consejo Nacional de Universidades (CNU) also limits curriculum updates, further centralizing authority and impacting the quality and independence of higher education institutions.

Dwight D. Eisenhower delegated much of the selection of his cabinet to Herbert Brownell Jr., who became his Attorney General and played a key role in shaping the administration. John Foster Dulles, a seasoned diplomat and corporate lawyer, was appointed Secretary of State, having previously advised prominent Republicans on foreign policy and built a reputation as a Cold War strategist. During his tenure, Dulles traveled over 500,000 miles, shaping U.S. foreign policy to counter Soviet influence. Eisenhower also established the role of White House Chief of Staff, appointing Sherman Adams to the position, and created the post of National Security Advisor, first held by Robert Cutler. Additionally, Eisenhower’s brother, Milton S. Eisenhower, served as an informal adviser, providing counsel on domestic and international issues.

On 7 April 2016, Kirk Penney signed a three-year deal to return to the New Zealand Breakers. He played his first game since 2011 on 7 October 2016, scoring 27 points in a 76–71 season-opening win over Melbourne United. Later that month, on 29 October, Penney scored 27 points in a 119–93 victory against the Adelaide 36ers. On 6 November, he dropped 30 points in an 86–70 win over the Brisbane Bullets. During the 2016–17 season, Penney appeared in 28 games, averaging 17.3 points, 3.6 rebounds, and 1.6 assists per game.

He was born in Buenos Aires, the son of Genaro Greco and Victoria Santo, a couple of modest means who brought up Vicente and seven other children. His family's low income caused Vicente to work from a young age; his schooling was interrupted, and he instead became a newsboy in the streets of Buenos Aires. Some of his siblings were also musically-inclined. Vicente's musical career began around 1903 during his teens, when he played guitar complemented by singing, for fun.

In October 2010, Clare Maguire began her European concert tour, supporting Hurts and Plan B. She collaborated with Birmingham rap artist Mike Skinner to write and record "Lock the Locks," featured on The Streets' 2011 album *Computers and Blues*. Additionally, she contributed vocals to "Midnight Caller" on Chase & Status's 2011 album *No More Idols*, showcasing her versatility across genres.

Frank Ocean embarked on a solo tour across North America and Europe to promote his 2011 mixtape *Nostalgia, Ultra*. "Thinkin Bout You" was performed at every show, including his set at Coachella in April 2012. Complex praised Ocean's falsetto on the track, noting its ability to captivate audiences. The song was also performed at the 2012 MTV Video Music Awards, further solidifying its impact. Ocean's live renditions showcased his vocal prowess and helped establish "Thinkin Bout You" as a standout in his discography.

The state has several beaches in its coastal districts such as Rushikonda, Mypadu, Suryalanka etc.; caves such as, Borra Caves, Indian rock-cut architecture depicting Undavalli Caves and the country's second longest caves- the Belum Caves. The valleys and hills include, Araku Valley, Horsley Hills, Papi Hills etc. Arma Konda peak located in Visakhapatnam district is the highest peak in Eastern Ghats.

Mirpur, Azad Kashmir, lies at an elevation of 459 meters above sea level and is linked to the main Peshawar-Lahore Grand Trunk Road at Dina Tehsil. It serves as the headquarters of Mirpur District, which comprises three subdivisions: Mirpur, Chaksawari, and Dadyal. The new city of Mirpur was built in the late 1960s and is situated on the banks of Mangla Lake. Recent developments include the completion of the Regency Hotel, while the government has proposed the construction of Mirpur International Airport to further enhance the city’s infrastructure and connectivity.

Before his freshman season, Lavoy Allen was selected to Temple University's basketball team, where he was expected to bolster their defense and rebounding. In his first college basketball game, Allen scored 10 points and grabbed nine rebounds in a loss to Tennessee. From November 18 through December 9, 2007, he scored in double figures in six consecutive games, showcasing his potential. On February 10, 2008, Allen recorded a season-high 20 points in an 80-70 overtime victory against UMass. However, he faced challenges, including being benched on February 17 against Dayton for missing a study assignment, highlighting the need for improved discipline.

On Meitei Language Day, the Patriotic Writers' Forum has been bestowing three literary awards annually since 2008 to honor authors of patriotic-themed short stories in Meitei literature. The awards—Pacha Meetei Literary Award, R Kathing Tangkhul Literary Award, and Dr. Saroj Nalini Parratt Literary Award—are open to writers from both inside and outside Manipur. Each award includes a cash incentive, citation, memento, and shawl, recognizing contributions to Manipuri literature and celebrating the spirit of patriotism through storytelling.

Upon its formation, Typhoon Ike passed 165 km (105 mi) southwest of Guam, prompting the island to be placed under the third Condition of Readiness level, the first such issuance since Typhoon Pamela in 1982. Despite Ike's one-minute sustained winds of 110 km/h (70 mi/h), its compact size mitigated damage. A station on Nimitz Hill recorded winds of 30 km/h (20 mi/h) with higher gusts, reflecting the storm's limited impact on the island during its formative stages.

Martin Schütz, a prominent theoretical chemist, focused on developing advanced computational methods for electronic structure theory, particularly for large molecular systems. During his tenure at Stuttgart, he built upon foundational work from the 1980s, advancing explicitly correlated methods like CCSD(T)-F12. In the early 2000s, Schütz employed local correlation techniques and extended these methods to systems involving triple substitutions, addressing limitations of earlier approaches that were restricted to small molecules due to computational inefficiency. His innovations, such as the orbital-specific virtual local triples correction (OSV-L(T)), overcame these challenges, enabling accurate and efficient calculations for larger molecules. These advancements significantly improved the applicability of quantum chemical methods to complex systems, including biomolecules and reaction mechanisms.

He was born in Lichfield in Staffordshire on 3 February 1847, the son of Thomas Hartley a portrait painter and his wife, Caroline Lockwood. He studied Science at Edinburgh University and Marburg in Germany. He married Mary Laffan in 1882 and they had one son John who was killed in the First World War.

Wilson was born in Grapeland, Texas in 1921, learning to play guitar and harmonica at an early age. Acquiring his first steel guitar sometime between the age of 12 and 18, Wilson performed at various Houston clubs. He served in the United States Army and became a private first class. After serving in the Army, Wilson decided to pursue a musical career.

Théophile de Giraud, a prominent figure in the antinatalist movement, co-created Non-Parents Day, celebrated alternately in Brussels and Paris between 2009 and 2015. In 2020, he covered the statue of Leopold II on horseback near the Royal Palace in Brussels with red paint to denounce Belgium's colonial past and the atrocities committed in the Congo Free State. That same year, he organized a denatalist event in Paris, continuing his advocacy for critical discussions on reproduction and societal values surrounding parenthood.

Positive-displacement compressors work by forcing air in a chamber whose volume is decreased to compress the air. Once the maximum pressure is reached, a port or valve opens and air is discharged into the outlet system from the compression chamber. Common types of positive displacement compressors are

The current Barabanki district was first established by the British upon their annexation of Oudh State in 1856. Originally, the district was known as Daryabad district because its headquarters were at Daryabad, but in 1859 they were relocated to Barabanki. The name "Barabanki" was chosen for the district's official name over "Nawabganj", then the more common name of the town, for two reasons: first, to avoid any possible confusion with other places calleed Nawabganj, and second, because the civil station was technically located outside of Nawabganj in the small revenue village of Barabanki. Previously, under the Nawabs of Awadh, the area that would become Barabanki district was divided between five chaklas: Daryabad-Rudauli, Ramnagar, Dewa-Jahangirabad, Jagdispur, and Haidargarh.

This song is an up-tempo that lists off various influential natives of the Southern United States who all embody a "southern voice." Many are referred to not by full name but direct allusion, including civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr. ("Dr. King") and NASCAR driver Dale Earnhardt ("Number Three"). Furthermore, Jack Daniel is incorrectly referred to as "Jack Daniels".

Josh Whitman was hired as athletic director for NCAA Division III Wisconsin–La Crosse in 2010, where Eagles teams won four national championships and 21 conference titles. In 2014, he became athletic director at Washington University in St. Louis.

Eugene Archer, in the September 15, 1960 edition of The New York Times wrote, "American-International, with good intentions of presenting a faithful adaption of Edgar Allan Poe's classic tale of the macabre...blithely ignored the author's style. Poe's prose style, as notable for ellipsis as imagery, compressed or eliminated the expository passages habitual to nineteenth-century fiction and invited the readers' imaginations to participate." He further opined, "Under the low-budget circumstances, Vincent Price and Myrna Fahey should not be blamed for portraying the decadent Ushers with arch affectation, nor Mark Damon held to account for the traces of Brooklynese that creep into his stiffly costumed impersonation of the mystified interloper."

Ruddy attended Xavier High School, Holy Cross College (A.B., 1959), served in the USMC, and received his Ph.D. in International Law from Cambridge University (1969). He also received a LL.B. in 1965 from Loyola University and a LL.M. in 1967 from New York University.

The company was founded in 2004 and located in Bend, Oregon due to public incentives which were offered, including state loans and grants of US$1.3M. In return for the grants and loans Epic promised to create 4,000 jobs. Epic Aircraft was founded by Rick Schrameck, experienced in the computer hardware and aviation composites industries.

Born in Miami, Florida, Turner moved to Paris, where he recorded Hard Twelve, produced by Dany Wild ("Max"). Afterward he began experimenting with a big band format. He spent much of 2007 and 2008 on the road with a nine-piece band, with which he recorded Imperial Pressure. Discordance said the album was of great class, and mixed hip-hop, jazz, rock, and electronic music in an intelligent manner. His third album, Rhyme Space Continuum, was praised for its "vocal acrobatics".

Matt's Scooter was named Pacer of the Year and best three-year-old in 1988. In the following year, he was again named best pacer and was awarded the title of Harness Horse of the Year. Matt's Scooter was inducted into the Canadian Horse Racing Hall of Fame and the United States Horse Racing Hall of Fame in 2006.

On January 24, 2014, Jersey Jack Pinball announced that Pat Lawlor would design their third release, featuring an original theme. Released on October 13, 2016, "Dialed In!" included innovative features like Bluetooth connectivity and a built-in camera, marking a milestone in pinball technology.

Musculoskeletal pathologies such as osteoarthritis of the knee or shoulder (rotator cuff) tendinopathy are aspects of normal human aging. Most humans eventually have evidence of these disease on imaging. In other words, they are diseases of senescence. In a sense, all humans are in the "pre-pathogenic period" for these diseases.

The manga adaptation of *Hell Girl*, illustrated by Miyuki Etoo, was serialized in Kodansha's *Nakayoshi* shōjo manga magazine starting in 2005. The first title, simply named *Hell Girl*, was published in seven volumes, with the first volume released on January 25, 2006, and the final volume on September 25, 2008. Del Rey Manga originally licensed the series, releasing the first volume in North America in 2008. The last three volumes were later compiled into an omnibus edition for release.

The majority of microbes in the gut reside in the large intestine, where the pH ranges from approximately 6.0 near the cecum to around 7.0 near the rectum. These bacteria, dominated by the phyla Firmicutes, Bacteroidetes, Actinobacteria, and Proteobacteria, perform functions like fermenting carbohydrates and synthesizing vitamins, complementing human digestive enzymes. Analysis of 124 human microbiomes revealed their diversity and critical role in nutrient extraction and metabolism.

Retaj Hotels and Hospitality is seeking to expand into the European and United States markets, with $5 billion expected to be invested in its European expansion. In March 2013, Vice Chairman and Managing Director Mohamed bin Johar al-Mohamed revealed to Gulf Times that Retaj is targeting mainland Europe, identifying openings and negotiating to start operations in London. In the United States, the group is focused on acquiring existing hotel outlets as part of its strategy to bring traditional Arabic hospitality to new audiences globally.

Scott was born in Dubuque, Iowa on October 13, 1863. She studied at the School of the Art Institute of Chicago, the Pennsylvania Academy of the Fine Arts, and the Académie Colarossi. Around 1890 Scott settled in New York City where she worked as an illustrator for the Century Publishing Company. In 1897 she moved to Rochester, New York where she began her teaching career at the Mechanics Institute. She taught there until 1913.

The Labor Movement wing of Religious Zionism, founded in 1921 under the Zionist slogan "Torah va'Avodah" (Torah and Labor), was called HaPoel HaMizrachi. It represented religiously traditional Labour Zionists, both in Europe and in the Land of Israel, where it represented religious Jews in the Histadrut. In 1956, Mizrachi, HaPoel HaMizrachi, and other religious Zionists formed the National Religious Party to advance the rights of religious Zionist Jews in Israel. The National Religious Party (NRP) operated as an independent political party until the 2003 elections. In the 2006 elections, the NRP merged with the National Union (HaIchud HaLeumi). In the 2009 elections, the Jewish Home (HaBayit HaYehudi) was formed in place of the NRP. Other parties and groups affiliated with religious Zionism are Gush Emunim, Tkuma, and Meimad. Kahanism, a radical branch of religious Zionism, was founded by Rabbi Meir Kahane, whose party, Kach, was eventually banned for racism. Today, Otzma Yehudit is the leading wing of this school of thought.

Zollikon has a population (as of 31 December 2020) of 13,311. As of 2007, 17.9% of the population was made up of foreign nationals. As of 2008 the gender distribution of the population was 47.3% male and 52.7% female. Over the last 10 years the population has grown at a rate of 5.9%. Most of the population (as of 2000) speaks German (85.9%), with English being second most common ( 3.0%) and Italian being third ( 2.4%).

On 12 April 2016, Penney signed a three-year deal with the New Zealand Breakers. On 7 October 2016, he played in his first game for the Breakers since 2011, scoring nine points in a 76–71 season-opening win over Melbourne United. On 29 October 2016, he scored 27 points in a 119–93 win over the Adelaide 36ers. On 6 November 2016, he scored 30 points in an 86–70 win over the Brisbane Bullets. He appeared in all 28 games for the Breakers in 2016–17, averaging 17.3 points, 3.4 rebounds and 2.4 assists per game.

Sarah Myerscough attended St Mary's Sixth Form College, Blackpool, from 1996 to 1998, where she gained her A-Levels. In 1999, she obtained a Foundation Diploma in Art and Design at Blackpool and The Fylde College before pursuing further studies at Lancaster University. She earned a Bachelor of Arts (Hons) degree in Fine Art in 2002. Her work featured in the Student Degree Show that same year, exhibited at the Yorkshire Sculpture Park in Wakefield in June 2002.

Cliff Lubwa p’Chong was born on 20th August 1946 in Gulu, Northern Uganda. He attended Sir Samuel Baker School in Gulu for his junior secondary and O'level education. He later qualified as a grade five teacher at the National Teachers College, Kyambogo, in 1968. After qualifying, he was first posted to St. Charles Lwanga, Koboko, in North-Western Uganda. P’Chong pursued higher education at Makerere University, where he attained a bachelor's degree in literature and linguistics in 1971. He furthered his studies at other institutions, including serving as a creative writing fellow at the University of Iowa. Later, he lectured in literature at the Institute of Teacher Education, Kyambogo, contributing significantly to the academic and literary fields.

On January 14, Cramer announced that he would run for North Dakota's seat in the United States House of Representatives for a third time in the 2010 election. In early 2010, he appeared at North Dakota town hall meetings, where he opposed the Affordable Care Act. Cramer attended numerous Tea Party rallies in North Dakota, speaking about energy, taxes, jobs and the U.S. Constitution. At the state Republican Party convention in March 2010, former House Majority Leader Rick Berg won the Republican congressional nomination; Berg was elected to Congress in November.

Feminist language reform focuses on addressing sexism in language, which often unconsciously reinforces gender inequalities. Some languages, like Spanish, explicitly gender words, while English uses gendered pronouns but genderless nouns. Feminist philosophers argue that English reflects male-centered norms, highlighting the need to create equitable language that represents women's perspectives and challenges patriarchal structures.

Scigliano played at the youth level in England with Stoke City F.C. In 1996, he returned to Canada to sign with the Vancouver 86ers in the USL A-League. After three seasons with Vancouver he played indoor soccer with Philadelphia KiXX in the National Professional Soccer League. In the 2001/2002 season he won the league championship. In 2004, he played with the Columbus Clan F.C. in the Vancouver Metro Soccer League, where he won the VMSL Golden Boot Award. In 2007, he won the British Columbia Provincial Soccer Championship, and also reached the finals of the Open Canada Cup.

In 1964, Leherb was nominated for the Venice Biennale with his Time Destruction Manifesto, but his participation was prevented by the new Minister of Education Theodor Piffl-Perčević (ÖVP) after a government reshuffle. The plan was to have a deep blue pavilion with dead pigeons, umbrellas and dolls stuck to the walls. An art scandal was looming, and the leading art journal in Paris "Arts et Loisirs" ran the headline "First scandal of the Venice Biennale! The German magazine "Stern" put the scandal on the front page: "Surrealist Leherb: no white mice for Venice" and was not sparing with outbursts against the "cultural country" Austria.

The codex contains 15 lessons from the Gospels, Acts of the Apostles, and Epistles, and three from the Old Testament lectionary (Evangelistarium, Apostolarium), with some lacunae. The text is written in Greek minuscule letters, on 177 paper leaves – for the New Testament – (21.3 cm by 14.5 cm), in one column per page, 26 lines per page. The initial letters are rubricated. The whole codex has 228 leaves. Two leaves numbered 20, two leaves numbered 21, 29r not numbered, 35r not numbered, 64r has two numbers, 66r has two numbers. It was written by several various hands. The initial letters are beautifully decorated.

Leon Goldfarb, a widower in the state of New York, applied for survivor benefits under the Social Security Act. Leon Goldfarb's late wife had worked as a secretary for New York City public schools for nearly twenty-five years and paid all of her social security taxes until her death in 1968. Upon his wife's death, Leon Goldfarb applied for survivor benefits but was denied. The relevant statute, 42 U.S.C § 402(f)(1)(D), mandated that surviving widowers must meet the burden of proving that they had been receiving over half of their financial support from their wives. The law made no such requirement for widows, who would be provided survivor benefits regardless of their dependency on their husbands.

There are many museums in Edmonton of various sizes. The largest is the Royal Alberta Museum (RAM), which was formerly known as the Provincial Museum of Alberta until it was renamed in honour of Queen Elizabeth II's 2005 Alberta centennial visit. The RAM houses over 10 million objects in its collection and showcases the culture and practices of the diverse aboriginal tribes of the region. In 2018, the building relocated from its location in Glenora to a new building in downtown on 103A Avenue and 97 Street. The museum held a grand opening event and gave out 40,000 free tickets for its first few days of operation.

The Tofane group consists of three main peaks: Tofana di Mezzo (3,244 m / 10,643 ft), the highest and third-highest in the Dolomites after Marmolada and Antelao; Tofana di Dentro (3,238 m / 10,623 ft); and Tofana di Rozes (3,225 m / 10,581 ft). Tofana di Mezzo was first climbed in 1863 by Paul Grohmann and Francesco Lacedelli, followed by Tofana di Rozes in 1864, with Grohmann joined by Lacedelli, Santo Siorpaes, and Angelo Dimai. Tofana di Dentro was conquered in 1865 by Grohmann and Dimai. These peaks are renowned for their dramatic landscapes and mountaineering routes, making them iconic features of the Dolomites.

Pratchett's Discworld novels have led to dedicated conventions, the first in Manchester in 1996, then worldwide, often with the author as guest of honour. Publication of a new novel was sometimes accompanied by an international book signing tour; queues were known to stretch outside the bookshop as the author continued to sign books well after the intended finishing time. His fans were not restricted by age or gender, and he received a large amount of fan mail from them. Pratchett enjoyed meeting fans and hearing what they think about his books, saying that since he was well paid for his novels, his fans were "everything" to him.

High Rocks, located within Ralph Stover State Park, rises dramatically above Tohickon Creek and is composed of red argillite from the Lockatong Formation and friable shales of the Brunswick Formation. The area features numerous climbing routes across 10 walls, with difficulties ranging from 5.0 to 5.12. The slippery texture of the argillite and the fragile nature of the shale make climbing challenging. Both traditional and sport climbing are available, with over 150 established routes for climbers to explore.

In response to the 2018 scandal involving five Democratic members of Cincinnati City Council—P.G. Sittenfeld, Greg Landsman, Tamaya Dennard, Chris Seelbach, and Wendell Young—who admitted to violating Ohio's Open Meetings Act, Vice Mayor Christopher Smitherman announced a ballot initiative in May 2018 to reduce council terms from four years to two. The initiative, known as "Issue 10," aimed to increase accountability by shortening term lengths. The measure was placed on the November 6, 2018, ballot through a vote of Cincinnati's governing officials. Cincinnati voters approved Issue 10 with 55.8% of the vote. The amendment, which changes council terms to two years, will take effect starting with the 2021 municipal elections.

The Church of the Resurrection (Rez) in Wheaton, Illinois, is renowned for its dynamic and theatrical worship services, blending ancient liturgy with artistic expression. Its Easter Vigil, described as the "crowning jewel of Holy Week," began under the leadership of Canon William Beasley in the 1980s, whose theater background shaped its creative and immersive nature. The service, lasting 2.5 to 3 hours, features four movements: the Service of Light, Readings, Baptism, and Eucharist, celebrating Christ’s resurrection and salvation history. In 2011, before acquiring its current building, Rez held the vigil at Glenbard West High School, later moving to Edman Chapel at Wheaton College, where a record 1,500 attendees gathered in 2017. Today, the Easter Vigil remains the church’s largest annual service, emphasizing Gospel catechesis and community participation.

Acute postoperative endophthalmitis caused by S. lugdunensis is infrequently reported in clinical studies. Five cases of acute postcataract surgery endophthalmitis were taken from a multicenter prospective study conducted in four university-affiliated hospitals in France (2004 to 2005). These cases were characterized by severe ocular inflammation occurring with a mean delay of 7.6 days after cataract surgery, severe visual loss (hand motions or less in three cases), and dense infiltration of the vitreous. Each of these patients was initially treated by using a standard protocol with intravitreal (vancomycin and ceftazidime), systemic, and topical antibiotics. Given the severity of the endophthalmitis, though bacteria were sensitive to intravitreal antibiotics, pars plana vitrectomy was needed in four cases. The final visual prognosis was complicated by severe retinal detachment in three cases. The microbiological diagnosis was reached by using conventional cultures with specific biochemical tests and eubacterial PCR amplification followed by direct sequencing.

El Rodeo School used to serve students in kindergarten through eighth grade. Beginning in 2019, El Rodeo serves students only in kindergarten through fifth grade. El Rodeo School was established in 1927 and enrolls 743 students on its campus at 605 Whittier Drive. The school is temporarily closed for reconstruction. The Principal was Kevin Allen, later principal of the middle school (see below) and announced his retirement in 2021. The Vice Principal was Kevin Painter, who later moved to Hawthorne (see below).

Popular alignment algorithms such as BLAST and MegaBLAST use a non-spaced model, where the entire length of the seed is made of exact matches. Thus, any mismatching base pair along the length of the seed will result in the program ignoring the potential hit. In a spaced model (such as PatternHunter), the matches are not necessarily consecutive.

John O'Dwyer first played for Tipperary at the age of 16, joining the minor team during the 2008 Munster Championship. He made his debut on April 30, 2008, scoring two points in a 3-21 to 1-12 victory over Limerick. Tipperary later lost the Munster final to Cork by 0-19 to 0-18, with O'Dwyer coming on as a substitute for David Butler.

On September 15, 1842, as a member of Captain John C. Hays's company of volunteers, Neighbors was in San Antonio attending Judge Anderson Hutchinson's court, when General Adrian Woll made his invasion of Texas and captured the city.: 15 Along with approximately fifty-two: 16 other individuals, including the officers of the court, he was forcibly marched to Mexico, where he was subsequently imprisoned in San Carlos Fortress.: 18

The 2018 City Gala was announced as part of the third annual event, held on Sunday, March 4, 2018, at Universal Studios Hollywood, California. Taking place as an Oscars after-party, the gala featured actor Matthew McConaughey as the keynote speaker and honored him as the Inspiration Award recipient for his work with the just keep livin Foundation. The event included a poker tournament, live auction, and other philanthropic activities.

Bonner began his collegiate career at Louisville, where he redshirted his true freshman season. He was dismissed from the team going into his redshirt freshman season after a female student accused him of having a gun. Following his dismissal, Bonner transferred to East Mississippi Community College, where he was featured in the second season of the Netflix documentary series Last Chance U.  In his lone season at East Mississippi, Bonner recorded 37 tackles, 5.5 sacks and two fumble recoveries and committed to transfer to Florida Atlantic University (FAU).

Newsarama ranked Gilgamesh, also known as "The Forgotten One," as the second worst Avengers member, highlighting his association with ancient Sumerian culture. The commentary humorously criticized his appearance, particularly the unusual "cow" he is depicted wearing on his head. They suggested that other mythological figures like Mithras or Marduk might have been better choices, further emphasizing the character's lack of prominence and appeal.

On July 31, 2011, Pomeranz, Alex White, Joe Gardner, and Matt McBride were traded to the Colorado Rockies for Ubaldo Jiménez. He made his Major League debut when he started and pitched five scoreless innings to pick up the win against the Cincinnati Reds on September 11, 2011. He made 4 starts in September 2011, finishing 1–2 with a 5.40 ERA.

Lasky wrote eight novels, five plays, three books of poetry and more than 50 screenplays, including eight for director Cecil B. DeMille. In addition to a Christopher Award, he was a two-time winner of the Boxoffice Magazine Award: in 1949 for Samson and Delilah, and in 1956 for The Ten Commandments. Lasky's writing career took him from Hollywood to London, Rome, Austria, Denmark, Turkey, Spain, Portugal, Greece and France. David Hempstead cowritted the script for Hell and High Water (1954) alongside Lasky.

The ideal candidate site for proposed wind power projects in Carrascal is located in the northern part of the study area, covering approximately 9 km² of ideal terrain with an average wind-power density capacity of 2400 W/m². The site is accessible via existing infrastructure, including roads and transmission lines, which enhance its attractiveness for energy development. The terrain is characterized by open, elevated areas that overlook surrounding landscapes, providing optimal conditions for wind energy generation. Observations of vegetation, particularly small trees and conifers, reveal permanent deformations, or "flagging," indicating strong prevailing winds from the north. Wind speeds measured at the site have reached up to 21.5 m/s, suggesting significant potential for energy production. However, while the data gathered provides promising insights, further validation studies, including on-site measurements and extended modeling, are necessary to confirm the reliability of the wind resource assessments. The presence of transmission infrastructure within the site further supports its feasibility for wind power development, reducing costs associated with energy transport.

At 16, Erwin Sak began playing regularly in senior teams. In 2006, he joined Drawa Thule Krzyż Wlkp. and, after six months, was promoted to the Polish fourth division, playing for Sokół Pniewy until January 2008. He then went on a one-week trial with Championship side Cardiff City and signed for the club. On 19 February 2008, Sak was included in Cardiff’s first team for the first time as a substitute in the FAW Premier Cup semi-final against Newport County.

Justice Stephen Breyer wrote the plurality opinion in *Colorado Republican Federal Campaign Committee v. FEC*, joined by Justices Ruth Bader Ginsburg, David Souter, and John Paul Stevens. Breyer relied on the precedent set in *Buckley v. Valeo*, emphasizing that some restrictions on party expenditures could violate First Amendment protections. He found that the Party Expenditure Provision of the FECA did not apply to independent expenditures. The case centered on the Colorado Republican Party's independent advertisement, which Breyer concluded was constitutionally protected as free speech.

Grandville Public Schools embraces the "be nice." program as a positive anti-bullying approach. Developed by Christy Buck, Executive Director of the Mental Health Foundation of West Michigan, the program promotes mental health awareness and suicide prevention. While Buck previously served on the Grandville School Board, "be nice." has also been adopted in schools across West Michigan.

Josua de Grave (1643–1712) was a Dutch artist best known for his detailed topographical drawings, primarily executed in pen and ink. Born in Amsterdam, he became a member of the Haarlem Guild of St. Luke in 1659. During 1667–1668, he created topographical drawings of the Netherlands and France, and between 1672–1674, he documented the military camps of Stadtholder William III. Later in his career, he also produced fantastical Italianate landscapes. De Grave’s work is often confused with that of his contemporaries, the brothers Valentijn and Barend Klotz, with whom he traveled. His drawings, which focused on capturing landscapes and everyday scenes, were sometimes attributed to Timotheus de Graaf, though their relationship remains unclear. De Grave’s meticulous style and use of pen and ink have made his works significant in the study of 17th-century Dutch art.

While residing in Birmingham, Eddowes began a relationship with former soldier Thomas Conway, who had served in the 18th Royal Irish Regiment and who received a small regimental pension. The couple had two children: Catherine Ann (b. 1863); and Thomas Lawrence (b. 1867), and initially supported themselves by selling chapbooks—frequently featuring gallows ballads—penned by Conway and occasionally via labouring work when his health permitted. No evidence exists to support the two having actually married following their initial acquaintance in 1861 or 1862, although shortly after the two began a relationship, Eddowes began referring to herself as "Kate Conway". She later had Conway's initials ("TC") crudely tattooed in blue ink on her left forearm.

The Newcastle Arena opened in November 1995 with the resident basketball team, the Newcastle Comets, who had been relocated from Sunderland, where they were previously known as the Sunderland Scorpions. In 1996, the team was bought by Sir John Hall and renamed the Newcastle Eagles for the start of the 1996–97 season, aligning with his Sporting Club project.

In the late 19th and early 20th century, Switzerland had become a popular place for winter vacations and people went there from all over Europe. Winter sports like skiing, sledding and bandy was played in Geneva and other towns. Students from Oxford and Cambridge went to Switzerland to play each other – the predecessor of the recurring Ice Hockey Varsity Match was a bandy match played in St. Moritz in 1885. This popularity for Swiss venues of winter sport may have been a reason, the European Championship was held there in 1913.

In 2007, Casey Cizikas was involved in a tragic incident during a youth rugby game in Mississauga, Ontario, where he tackled 15-year-old Manny Castillo, lifting and driving him headfirst into the ground. Cizikas described the tackle as part of the game, but Castillo, who had suffered a concussion two weeks earlier, succumbed to a fatal head injury two days later. A doctor testified that the prior concussion made Castillo more vulnerable to severe injury. Despite the tragedy, the Castillo family expressed compassion for Cizikas, with Manny’s father questioning why charges were brought against him. Cizikas, 16 at the time, was convicted of manslaughter, though Peel Regional Police emphasized that the conviction was not a reflection of his character but rather the result of the incident’s outcome. The case, which deeply impacted both families, became a defining moment in Cizikas’ early life.

The finale of William Walton's Cello Concerto, in A minor, is the third of its three movements and follows a theme-and-variations structure with four improvisations—two for orchestra and two for solo cello. After the improvisations, the movement reverts to the reflective mood of the first movement. The solo cello introduces an elaborate theme in its high register, characterized by melancholy harmonies and lush Romantic melodies, while the opening bars present a subdued, lyrical quality that sets the tone for the finale.

In the 19th century, a branch of the Wilts & Berks Canal was built to Chippenham, terminating at Chippenham Wharf on Timber Street, where coal was the main commodity traded. The wharf site is now occupied by a car park. The canal's decline began after the Great Western Railway arrived in 1841, boosting Chippenham’s growth as a transport hub and leading to the canal's eventual closure in 1912. The railway spurred industrial and residential expansion, with the town growing significantly in the 20th century, particularly into the surrounding countryside, supported by new housing developments and a network of A-roads and roundabouts.

Digifone was created in 2000, as the demerged mobile business of the prior Esat Digifone which had just been purchased by BT. Esat Digifone was originally a joint venture between Esat Telecom and Telenor. After the acquisition of Esat Telecom by BT the Telenor share in Esat Digifone was also acquired by BT. Subsequently, the fixed operations, Esat Telecom later became BT Ireland and the demerged mobile operations, rebranded without the Esat name as Digifone, became a part of the newly formed separate company, mmO2 plc. Digifone became O2 Ireland in 2001 as a subsidiary of mmO2 plc following the demerger from BT. O2 Ireland became a subsidiary of Telefónica in 2006, after its parent company O2 in the United Kingdom was purchased. In June 2013, Hutchison Whampoa announced it would acquire the Irish arm of O2 for €780m. O2 was merged into Hutchison Whampoa's subsidiary Three Ireland in March 2015.

Sky and BBC News reported that the East London Mosque on Whitechapel Road in Whitechapel had been surrounded by armed police and that residents were told to stay indoors. The mosque was evacuated at about 10:30 and searched. However, police confirmed that it was a bomb scare and the all-clear was given after just over an hour.

"Piece of Me" received widespread acclaim from music critics, many of whom considered it a standout track from Britney Spears's fifth studio album, *Blackout*. Alex Fletcher of *Digital Spy* awarded the song four stars, describing it as "a two fingered-salute to the media hounds" and praising its defiant tone. Fletcher also noted that the opening line "poops from a great height on anything Lily Allen has ever penned," highlighting its sharp wit. Peter Robinson of *The Observer* and Margeaux Watson of *Entertainment Weekly* both named "Piece of Me" as one of the standout tracks of the album, while Dennis Lim of *Blender* called it one of the best tracks on *Blackout* alongside "Gimme More." Laura Herbert of *BBC News* went further, declaring it "without doubt the best track on the whole album" and a "masterpiece." Writing for *The New York Times*, Kelefa Sanneh praised the production by Bloodshy & Avant, stating that they "evoke the horror, the exhilaration and (finally) the boredom of [Spears's] overexamined life," calling the result "brilliant." The song's biting lyrics and electro-thudding production were widely celebrated as a bold response to the intense media scrutiny surrounding Spears's personal life.

The 2014 New Zealand general election was held on 20 September, returning John Key's National Government for a third term. The National Party won 47.0% of the party vote and secured 60 of the 121 seats in Parliament, one fewer than the 61 seats it appeared to hold on election night. The lost list seat went to the Green Party on the official count. Following the election, the National Government re-entered confidence and supply agreements with ACT, United Future, and the Māori Party to maintain its majority.

During his early years, Steve Winwood attended Great Barr School while immersing himself in the Birmingham blues rock scene. Living on Atlantic Road in Great Barr, close to the city's music halls, he played piano, organ, and guitar, backing blues and rock legends like Muddy Waters, John Lee Hooker, Howlin’ Wolf, and B.B. King on their UK tours—a common practice for US singers who relied on local musicians. Winwood modeled his distinctive high tenor voice on Ray Charles, earning praise for his ability to emulate the American R&B icon, a skill that set him apart among young British musicians.

The major shareholder of Fosun International was Fosun Holdings, a Hong Kong-incorporated company, fully owned by Fosun International Holdings, which was incorporated in the British Virgin Islands. Fosun International Holdings was owned by founders Guo Guangchang (58%), Liang Xinjun (22%), and Wang Qunbin (20%). Fan Wei, another co-founder, withdrew his stake in Fosun International Holdings in 2004.

In the Baskin-Robbins murders, forensic evidence from the victims was found in Paul Dennis Reid's car, along with proof of a credit card gasoline purchase near the location of the bodies on the night of the crime, placing him at the scene approximately 40 miles (64 km) from his home. Witnesses reported seeing a vehicle resembling Reid's in the immediate area at the time. Blood evidence from the victims was also discovered on his shoes. Reid was found guilty on two counts of first-degree murder. The trial for these murders took place in Clarksville, occurring between the two Nashville trials.

In September 1929, the Sacred Congregation for Seminaries and Universities in Rome granted a charter to Saint Mary, allowing it to grant pontifical academic degrees. In 1934, this authority became permanent. Saint Mary became the first pontifical university in the United States. Monsignor Reynold Henry Hillenbrand served as rector of St. Mary from 1936 to 1944.

The Japanese government's official view has evolved since 1905. On January 28, 1905, the Cabinet reached a decision (明治三十八年一月二十八日閣議決定 lit. 'Cabinet decision on January 28, Meiji 38') to incorporate Liancourt Rocks, on grounds that it had been terra nullius under international law: "There is no recognizable trace that this uninhabited island was ever occupied by a foreign country ... As evidenced through relevant records that a person by the name of Nakai Yozaburo (中井養三郞) moved to said island in 1903 (Meiji 36) and practiced fishing there since, we recognise the fact that occupation has occurred in terms of international law."

After the end of the Second World War, the squadron replaced its Lancasters with Avro Lincolns, following those in 1952 with the English Electric Canberra jet bomber. The squadron was deployed to Malaya for four months in 1955, returning to RAF Binbrook to be disbanded on 15 December 1955. Reformed at RAF Scampton on 1 May 1958 as part of RAF Bomber Command's V-bomber force maintaining the UK's strategic nuclear deterrent, the squadron was equipped with the Avro Vulcan B1 from Aug 1960. By 23 May 1961, its aircraft were the upgraded Vulcan B1A fitted with the electronic countermeasures tail pod.
